evidence_index.verbatim_quote: On May 27, 2015, Keiper performed on plaintiff L4-5 microlaminotomies and medial facetectomies, resection of bilateral synovial cysts, and, particularly relevant to this appeal, an operation that fused two of plaintiff’s vertebrae with a titanium cage and bilateral pedicle screws.
evidence_index.summary: Keiper performed spinal surgery on May 27, 2015, including vertebrae fusion.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.verbatim_quote: When plaintiff awoke from surgery, he did not have the strength to raise his left foot. Plaintiff was told that he had 'foot drop,' and Peterson, Keiper’s physician assistant, reassured plaintiff that the foot drop would soon get better.
evidence_index.summary: Post-surgery, plaintiff experienced foot drop and was reassured by physician assistant.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.verbatim_quote: Dietrich testified that the pedicle screw was responsible for damaging the L4 and L5 nerve roots. He further testified that, the sooner that the source of an injury to a nerve root is removed, the more likely that the injury will abate.
evidence_index.summary: Expert Dietrich testified that the pedicle screw damaged nerve roots and early removal could have improved the condition.
evidence_index.speaker: Expert witness

evidence_index.verbatim_quote: The trial court erred in granting a directed verdict on the ground that plaintiff had not proven causation.
evidence_index.summary: Trial court's directed verdict on causation was erroneous.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

evidence_index.verbatim_quote: A jury could have found that a pedicle screw left in a position to impinge a nerve root is likely to cause lasting injury, and, given Dietrich’s testimony and the record, plaintiff provided proof of the injury caused by defendants’ negligence with sufficient certainty for plaintiff’s claim to reach the jury.
evidence_index.summary: Court reasoned that evidence was sufficient for jury consideration.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's analysis

evidence_index.verbatim_quote: Dietrich concluded his testimony by stating that defendants’ failure to obtain appropriate imaging studies at the time of plaintiff’s surgery to ascertain whether a pedicle screw was misplaced and was damaging the nerve root fell below the standard of care.
evidence_index.summary: Dietrich testified that failure to obtain imaging studies fell below standard of care.
evidence_index.speaker: Expert witness

initial_state.history_of_present_illness: Patient underwent spinal surgery including vertebrae fusion with a titanium cage and bilateral pedicle screws.

requestables.name: Post-operative imaging
requestables.description: Imaging to check placement of pedicle screws

reveal.clinical_significance: Could have identified misplaced pedicle screw
reveal.decision_impact: Failure to perform imaging delayed identification of nerve root impingement

decision_points.clinical_context: Patient with foot drop post spinal surgery
decision_points.prompt: What should be the next step in management?

options.description: Order post-operative imaging to check screw placement
options.clinical_reasoning: Imaging would confirm or rule out screw impingement on nerve root
Assumed foot drop would resolve without further investigation

actual_action_defendant.description: Defendant reassured patient and did not order imaging

expected_action_court.description: Post-operative imaging should have been ordered
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert testimony indicated imaging was necessary

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Failure to order imaging fell below standard of care
explanation.what_should_have_happened: Imaging should have been performed to assess screw placement

patient_outcome.description: Patient experienced lasting foot drop due to nerve root impingement

key_findings: Breach of duty in failing to order imaging
Causation established - earlier imaging could have prevented lasting injury

malpractice_determination.counterfactual: Imaging would have identified screw impingement

diagnoses.diagnosis: Foot drop due to nerve root impingement
